difficulty_factors: Silk is a protein fiber — sensitive to alkaline agents, enzymes, heat, and water spotting
Butter is combination: milk fat (triglyceride) + trace milk protein + possible salt
Dry cleaning strongly preferred — wet treatment risks permanent water marks on silk

diy.title: Butter on Silk
mixSteps: Mix 1 drop Dawn in 1 cup cool water — silk needs extremely dilute surfactant
Prepare vinegar rinse: 1 tbsp white vinegar per cup cool water

steps.instruction: Gently scrape any solid butter with a dull knife or spoon edge — lift away, don't smear.
steps.why: Removing solid material first minimizes the stain footprint. Butter is semi-solid — scraping removes the bulk.

steps.instruction: Apply cornstarch or talcum powder generously. Wait 15-20 minutes.
steps.why: Powder adsorbs melted butter fat without any chemical risk to silk. This is your safest, most effective first step.

steps.instruction: Brush off gently. If stain is lighter, repeat powder step. If greasy residue persists, move to step 4.
steps.why: Multiple powder applications are safer on silk than one chemical application.

steps.instruction: Dab the extremely dilute soap solution onto the remaining stain. Press gently — NEVER rub silk.
steps.why: The trace amount of surfactant emulsifies remaining butter fat.

steps.instruction: Rinse by dabbing with cool water. Immediately follow with the vinegar rinse.
steps.why: Vinegar restores silk's natural acidic pH (4-6) and prevents dulling from soap residue.

steps.instruction: Lay flat on a clean towel to air dry. Never wring, twist, or use heat.
steps.why: Heat sets butter fat. Wringing damages silk fiber. Flat drying preserves shape.

diy.ifStillThere: Take to a professional dry cleaner — specify butter on silk and ask for POG pre-spotting before the GreenEarth or hydrocarbon cleaning cycle. This is one case where professional care is clearly worth it.
proTips: Scrape solid butter first — then powder — then chemistry. That order is key.
Powder before chemicals is the golden rule for oil on silk.
Butter contains trace milk protein, but on silk DO NOT use protease enzyme — silk IS protein.
diy.safety: Test any solution on a hidden seam first. Silk dyes bleed easily. Never use chlorine bleach, ammonia, or enzyme products on silk. Never use hot water.
diy.whyThisWorks: Butter is primarily milk fat (triglyceride) with trace casein protein and salt. On silk, the fat component dominates treatment. Scraping removes solids. Powder absorbs melted fat. Dilute surfactant emulsifies the residue. The protein trace is too small to require separate treatment, and you can't use protease on silk anyway — silk IS protein fiber.

home.title: Butter on Silk — Careful Removal
home.confidenceNote: Fresh butter responds to scraping + powder. Aged melted butter may need professional dry cleaning.

primaryProduct.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)
primaryProduct.why: Solvent-based — safer for silk than water-heavy methods
primaryProduct.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils fat cooking oil

alternativeProducts.name: Dawn Ultra Dish Soap (Blue)
alternativeProducts.why: Use extremely diluted (1 drop per cup cool water) on silk only

alternativeProducts.name: The Laundress Stain Solution
alternativeProducts.why: Formulated for delicate fabrics including silk

home.prepStep: Scrape solid butter with dull knife. Apply cornstarch for 15-20 minutes before any wet treatment.

method.instruction: Scrape solid butter gently. Apply cornstarch for 15-20 minutes. Brush off.
method.why: Physical removal + absorption handles the bulk of butter without chemistry.

method.instruction: Apply Carbona #5 per label. Test on hidden seam first.
method.why: Solvent-based formula dissolves butter fat without water-spotting silk.

method.instruction: Blot gently with clean white cloth.
If residue persists and garment is hand-washable: brief hand wash in cool water with 1 drop mild soap. Rinse immediately. Vinegar rinse to restore pH.
method.why: Brief wet contact only. Vinegar restores silk's natural acidic pH.

method.instruction: Lay flat on a clean towel to dry. Roll gently to extract moisture.
method.why: Heat sets fat. Wringing damages silk.

materialWarnings.warning: Silk weakens 20% when wet. Handle with extreme care — no rubbing, no wringing, no heat.
NEVER use protease enzyme on silk — silk IS a protein fiber. Protease digests silk.
Water spotting is a real risk. Minimize wet contact. Use distilled water.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: Take to a professional dry cleaner. Specify butter on silk. Ask for GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent.

proTips: Scrape → powder → solvent. That sequence is your best path on silk.
Don't panic about the trace protein in butter — it's too small to treat separately on silk.
Professional dry cleaning is often the best value for butter on silk — saves time and risk.
home.whyThisWorks: Butter is 80% milk fat. Scraping removes solids. Powder absorbs liquid fat. Solvent dissolves residual grease. On silk, we keep water contact to an absolute minimum.
home.timeEstimate: 20-30 minutes (professional follow-up recommended for aged stains)

pro.title: Butter on Silk — Professional Dry-Side Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (dairy fat — triglyceride). Butter is ~80% milk fat (triglycerides of palmitic, oleic, and stearic acids), ~16% water, ~2% milk solids (casein protein), ~2% salt. On silk, the fat component is dominant. The trace casein protein is too small for separate treatment — and protease enzyme is forbidden on silk (silk IS protein). Treatment: scrape solids, POG pre-spot to dissolve fat, gentle solvent cycle. Dry-side throughout — water spotting on silk is often a bigger problem than the original stain.

spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle scraping with bone spatula or spoon
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid butter from the surface — lift away, don't press in. For butter that has melted and absorbed, skip directly to absorbent powder or POG.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG or Pyratex® POG
spottingProtocol.technique: Dry-side POG pre-spot for milk fat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place silk face-down on clean white towel. Apply POG to back of stain with dauber. Light tamping with bone spatula — silk fiber abrades easily. Watch towel for fat transfer. Move to fresh position and repeat. Feather edges with VDS (Formula 83) to prevent solvent ring on silk's smooth surface. Butter fat is easier for POG than motor oil — 2 rounds usually sufficient.

GreenEarth is ideal for silk — rated A, no odor, preserves luster. Hydrocarbon also rated A. The POG pre-spot dissolved concentrated fat; the cleaning cycle handles residual. Use silk-safe detergent charge.
Hold silk to light. Fat residue shows as translucent dark area. If clear, proceed to finishing. If halo visible, repeat POG and re-clean before pressing.

pro.escalation: Butter fat is relatively easy for POG to dissolve — escalation rarely needed. If aged and oxidized: amyl acetate. The trace protein component doesn't typically require treatment on silk since protease is forbidden.
pro.customerExplanation: Butter on silk needs professional solvent treatment to remove the milk fat without risking water marks or color damage. We pre-spotted with a degreasing agent and cleaned with a silk-safe solvent. The butter is gone and the silk's natural luster is preserved.
commonMistakes: Using water-based cleaning — water spots on silk can be harder to remove than the butter stain itself.
Using protease enzyme for butter's protein component — protease digests silk fiber. NEVER use on silk.
Heavy mechanical action on silk — light taps only.
Skipping VDS feathering — leaves visible ring on silk's smooth surface.
pro.neutralization: Purely dry-side treatment — no neutralization needed.
pro.fabricRisks: Same as cooking oil on silk: dye bleeding, water spotting, seam slippage, fiber abrasion. GreenEarth is the safest solvent choice.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG Remover
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson NovaBone POG remover professional spotting
professional.note: Primary POG agent for butter fat. Dissolves triglycerides on contact.

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Formula 83 VDS
professional.amazonSearchQuery: AL Wilson Formula 83 volatile dry solvent
professional.note: Essential for edge feathering on silk. Prevents ring marks.

household.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)
household.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils fat cooking oil
household.note: Consumer-grade solvent. Minimizes water contact on silk.

household.name: Cornstarch
household.note: Safest first step after scraping. Zero chemical risk.

household.name: Dawn Ultra Dish Soap (Blue)
household.note: Extremely diluted (1 drop per cup cool water) only. Last resort for silk.

title: Butter on Silk
stainChemistry: Butter is primarily milk fat — triglycerides of palmitic, oleic, and stearic acids — with trace milk protein (casein) and salt. On silk, the fat is the treatment target. Butter is semi-solid at room temperature, so it starts as a surface deposit but melts with body heat and penetrates along silk's smooth filaments. The trace protein in butter is too small to warrant separate treatment, and critically, protease enzyme (the standard protein stain tool) is FORBIDDEN on silk because silk IS a protein fiber. Treatment is purely dry-side: mechanical removal, POG for fat dissolution, gentle solvent cleaning.
whyThisWorks: POG dissolves butter fat through like-dissolves-like solvency. The dry-side approach protects silk from water spotting. GreenEarth preserves silk's natural luster. The combination of mechanical removal + absorbent powder + POG handles butter comprehensively without any of the agents that damage silk.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solids. Apply powder generously. Wait 15-20 minutes. Brush off. Repeat if stain lightens.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board, clean towels, bone spatula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Face-down on clean towel. POG to back of stain. Light tamping. Feather with VDS. Butter fat is straightforward for POG.
GreenEarth ideal for silk. HC also excellent. Silk-safe detergent.
Confirm no fat halo before any heat touches the silk.

homeSolutions: Scrape + cornstarch: Remove solid butter, apply powder, wait 15-20 minutes. Repeat. Often sufficient for fresh butter on silk.
Carbona Stain Devils #5: Solvent-based approach after scraping and powder. Test on hidden area first.
Extremely dilute Dawn (1 drop per cup cool water): Dab on, don't rub. Rinse with cool water. Vinegar rinse for pH restoration.
Professional dry cleaning: For silk, this is often the safest and most effective choice.
materialWarnings: NEVER use protease enzyme on silk — silk IS protein. Protease digests silk fiber.
NEVER use hot water, chlorine bleach, or ammonia on silk.
NEVER rub or wring silk. Press and dab only.
Water spotting is a real risk — minimize wet contact.
commonMistakes: Using enzyme detergent because 'butter has protein' — correct observation, wrong solution on silk. Protease destroys silk.
Water before solvent — causes water spotting on silk and spreads the fat.
Rubbing — silk abrades when wet or solvent-laden. Light taps only.
scienceNote: Butter's milk fat is primarily saturated (palmitic C16:0, stearic C18:0) and monounsaturated (oleic C18:1). Saturated fats are solid at room temperature and semi-crystalline — they're actually easier for POG to dissolve than polyunsaturated cooking oils because they haven't cross-linked. Butter fat on silk is a cleaner problem than aged vegetable oil on silk because it's less prone to oxidative polymerization.
customerExplanation: Butter on silk needs delicate professional treatment. The milk fat was dissolved with a specialized solvent, and your garment was cleaned in our silk-safe system. No water marks, no color change — just clean silk.
escalation: Butter rarely requires escalation past POG. If aged and yellowed: amyl acetate. If pressed with iron before treatment: oxidized fat — H₂O₂ with extreme caution on silk (test first, brief contact only).
callPro: For butter on silk, professional dry cleaning is recommended in most cases. Silk's sensitivity to water and chemicals makes home treatment risky. A professional with GreenEarth solvent handles this routinely.
solventNote: POG pre-spot on dry side before cleaning cycle. GreenEarth preferred for silk. HC also rated A. PERC rated C for silk — use with caution.
